records.ety: [AS. maest, fem.; akin to G. mast, and E. meat. See Meat.]

defs: The fruit of the oak and beech, or other forest trees; nuts; acorns.
quotes: Oak mast, and beech, . . . they eat.
Swine under an oak filling themselves with the mast.

records.ety: [AS. maest, masc.; akin to D., G., Dan., & Sw. mast, Icel. mastr, and perh. to L. malus.]

defs: A pole, or long, strong, round piece of timber, or spar, set upright in a boat or vessel, to sustain the sails, yards, rigging, etc. A mast may also consist of several pieces of timber united by iron bands, or of a hollow pillar of iron or steel.
quotes: The tallest pine Hewn on Norwegian hills, to be the mast Of some great ammiral.<--sic-->
notes: ☞ The most common general names of masts are foremast, mainmast, and mizzenmast, each of which may be made of separate spars.

defs: The vertical post of a derrick or crane.
A spar or strut to which tie wires or guys are attached for stiffening purposes.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Masted; p. pr. & vb. n. Masting.]

defs: To furnish with a mast or masts; to put the masts of in position; as, to mast a ship.
